questions.question: What does the softmax function in attention do?
options: Converts raw attention scores into probabilities that sum to 1
Amplifies high values and suppresses low values
questions.explanation: Softmax converts attention scores into a probability distribution - each weight is between 0 and 1, and all weights sum to 1. This allows the model to interpret attention as a weighted average.

questions.question: In the attention formula Attention = softmax(Q·K^T/√d)·V, why do we divide by √d?
options: To reduce the variance of dot products and keep attention stable
To match the dimension of the output
To prevent the softmax from saturating
To normalize the query and key vectors
questions.explanation: When dot products are too large, softmax becomes very peaked (one value ≈1, others ≈0), losing information. Dividing by √d keeps the distribution spread out and trainable.

questions.question: Explain how multi-head attention works. Why use multiple heads instead of one large attention head?

rubric.complete: Mentions multiple parallel attention heads, each with independent Q/K/V projections, learning different relationships, then concatenated
rubric.partial: Mentions multiple heads and some benefit like learning different patterns

questions.question: What is causal attention (or masked attention)?
options: Attention where future tokens can attend to past tokens
Attention where past tokens cannot attend to future tokens (used in language models)
questions.explanation: In language models, we prevent tokens from attending to future tokens (which haven't been generated yet). This is implemented by masking future positions to -∞ before softmax, forcing attention weights to 0.

questions.question: Describe what Query, Key, and Value vectors represent conceptually.

rubric.complete: Q=what token is looking for, K=what each token offers, V=actual information to retrieve
